content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 24, 2017

Larissa Seto
Assistant City Attorney
P.O. Box 520
Pleasanton, CA 94566-0802

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-17-048

Dear Ms. Seto:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Pleasanton Planning
Commissioner Jack Balch regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Please note that this advice does not analyze any other area of law, including Section
1090, the Public Contract Code, or common law conflicts of interest. Please also note that we are
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

     Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Balch from taking part in the governmental decision on
whether the GHC Lund Ranch II Project's tentative map complies with the City's Planned Unit
Development Plan zoning for the Project given that a source of income to the Commissioner owns
real property nearby the Project?

CONCLUSION

     No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in that decision because it
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioner's source-of-
income interest in real property nearby the Project.

_______________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-048
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the Assistant City Attorney and the authorized representative of Commissioner
Balch, and you provided additional facts on March 6, 2017.

The City Council approved the GHC Lund Ranch II Project (the "Project") in January 2016.
As approved, the Project, located at 1500 Lund Ranch Road, would include 43 homes on 195 acres,
with 172 of those acres preserved as open space. Also in January 2016, the City Council enacted
Ordinance No. 2133 (the "Ordinance"), establishing Planned Unit Development Plan zoning solely
applicable to the Project's real-property parcels. The City Council determined that the Project's
zoning was in conformance with the City's General Plan when it adopted the Ordinance.

The City's Planning Commission will soon consider whether the Project's tentative map
complies with the Ordinance's zoning for the real-property parcels within the Project.

You state that Commissioner Balch has received more than $500 of income in the prior 12
months from a business entity, and that Sherman Balch, the Commissioner's uncle, has the power to
direct, or cause the direction of, that business entity's management and policies.

Sherman Balch and his wife own their residence at 6002 Alisal Street (the "Alisal
Property"). The Alisal Property is 6.431 acres, approximately 1,800 feets from the nearest proposed
new home within the Project, and about 737 feets from the Project's nearest property line. At least
three intervening parcels, Minnie Street, and Sycamore Creek Way all separate the Alisal Property
from the southwest corner of the Project. The dominate feature in the foreground of the Alisal
Property's existing view of the Project area is a City water tank, and the construction of the Project
will not affect that view.

The Project's Draft Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") concluded that the impact of
traffic increases resultant from the Project would be less than significant with planned mitigation.
(EIR, July 2014, 4.6-1 to 4.6-7.) The intersection closest to the Alisal Property is a three-way
intersection of Alisal Street, Minnie Street, and Sycamore Road. You state that that intersection was
not studied as part of the EIR because it is too far away from Lund Ranch Road and Sunset Creek
Lane, the street connections to the Project, to warrant consideration. And a driver going to or from
the Alisal Property does not need to use either Lund Ranch Road or Sunset Creek Lane.

With respect to the parking, the Project's 43 single-family homes would each have a
driveway and a garage, and there is street parking available within the Project area. You state that
the City does not anticipate that additional parking needs resultant from the Project will affect areas
outside the Project area.

The Project site is currently open space, and its construction will generally increase the
intensity of use of the nearby neighborhoods' roads and infrastructure. However, with respect to the
Project's effect on the local environment, the EIR concluded that the project would not result in any
significant environmental impacts that could not be mitigated to a less-than-significant level with
recommended mitigation measures. (EIR, supra, 2-2.)

File No. A-17-048
Page No. 3

The EIR concluded that the Project would result in temporary noise increases and air-quality
reductions due to the construction of the Project, and permanent noise increases and air-quality
reductions from the operation of the Project's residences. (EIR, supra, 4.7-1 to 4.9-4.) However, the
EIR also concluded that all noise and air-quality impacts from the Project would be less than
significant with mitigation. (Ibid.)

The Alisal Property is located within the area of the City's Happy Valley Specific Plan. The
western portion of the Alisal Property is pre-zoned as semi-rural density residential (PUD-SRDR),
and the eastern portion as agriculture / open space (PUD-AG/OS). The Happy Valley Specific Plan
authorizes a density of one home per two acres in areas pre-zoned PUD-SRDR, but does not allow
residential development in areas pre-zoned PUD-AG/OS. Therefore, you state that Sherman Balch
currently has the right to develop 1 home per 2 acres on the western portion of the Alisal Property.

The Ordinance's zoning applicable to the Project is only applicable to the Project, and will
not affect the Alisal Property's zoning. You also state that, in the future, if Sherman Balch seeks
more density with respect to the Alisal Property than is currently authorized under the Happy
Valley Specific Plan, he would need to obtain amendments to the General Plan, Happy Valley
Specific Plan, and Planned Unit Development Plan zoning applicable to the Alisal Property.

Although the EIR notes that the Project will extend Lund Ranch Road and Sunset Creek
Lane to the Project site as well as utilities, it also provides that "[r]oad and infrastructure
improvements would not extend to any undeveloped properties and thereby, not induce new
development." (EIR, supra, 2-2.)

A local real estate broker, Mike Carey (BRE #01055545), advised the Commissioner in
writing that the Project will have no impact on the value of the Alisal Property.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Section 87103(c) provides that an official has an interest in any source of income
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, or received by, the official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.² Regulation 18700.1(a)(2) provides that, in
addition to a source-of-income interest in any business entity from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within the 12 months before the relevant decision is made, the official also
has a source-of-income interest in any individual who has the power to direct or cause the direction
of the management and policies of the business entity.

² Gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms available
to the public without regard to official status are not source-of-income interests under the Act. (Section 87103(c).)

File No. A-17-048
Page No. 4

You state that the Commissioner has received more than $500 of income in the prior 12 months from a business entity, and that Sherman Balch has the power to direct, or cause the direction of, that business entity's management and policies. Based on these facts, the Commissioner has a source-of-income interest in Sherman Balch as an individual.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The effect of a decision before the official's agency is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if it is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Ibid.) For an interest not explicitly involved in a decision, the decision's effect is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

The Commissioner's source-of-income interest in Sherman Balch is not explicitly involved in the decision regarding the Project's zoning because neither Sherman Balch nor the Alisal Property are a named party in, or a subject of, that decision. Therefore, the effect of the decision is reasonably foreseeable if it is a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Regulation 18702.3 provides that a decision's effect on a source of income is material if the official knows or has reason to know that the decision will have a material financial effect on a real-property interest of the individual source of income under the real-property materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18702.2.

As is pertinent to the facts presented, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a real-property interest is material if the effect:

"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel of real property; . . .

"(10) Would change the character of the of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; . . .

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property."

Therefore, the Act would prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in the decision if any of these three real-property materiality standards is met with respect to the Commissioner's source-of-income interest in the Alisal Property.

File No. A-17-048
Page No. 5

Development Potential

We first examine whether the decision would affect the development potential of the Alisal
Property. You state that Sherman Balch currently has the right to develop 1 home per 2 acres on the
western portion of the Alisal Property under the City's Happy Valley Specific Plan.

The Planning Commission's decision on whether the Project's tentative map complies with
the Ordinance's Planned Unit Development Plan zoning for the Project will not affect the
development potential of the Alisal Property. The Ordinance's zoning is solely applicable to the
Project, and does not affect the Alisal Property's zoning. Furthermore, you also state that, in the
future, if Sherman Balch seeks more density with respect to the Alisal Property than is currently
authorized under the Happy Valley Specific Plan, he would need to obtain amendments to the
General Plan, Happy Valley Specific Plan, and the zoning applicable to the Alisal Property.

Furthermore, infrastructure improvements arising undertaken for the Project also will not
affect the development potential of the Alisal Property. Although the EIR notes that the Project will
extend Sunset Creek Way and Lund Ranch Road and utilities to the Project site, it also provides that
"[r]oad and infrastructure improvements would not extend to any undeveloped properties and
thereby, not induce new development." The extension of Sunset Creek Way and Lund Ranch Road
to the Project will primarily serve Project residents; those roads are both multiple residential blocks
north of the Alisal Property, and there are more convenient routes available to drive to and from the
Alisal Property. And the Alisal Property is served by existing utility lines and water tanks that are
not associated with the Project.

Therefore, based on the facts presented, the decision regarding the Project's zoning will not
affect the development potential of the Alisal Property.

Traffic Levels and Intensity of Use

We now examine whether the decision regarding the Project's zoning would change the
character of the Alisal Property by altering traffic levels or intensity of use of property surrounding
the Alisal Property, including parking, the view, privacy, noise levels, air quality, including odors,
or any other factors that would affect the market value of the Alisal Property.

The Project's EIR concluded that the impact of traffic increases resultant from the Project
would be less than significant with planned mitigation, and a driver going to or from the Alisal
Property does not need to use either Lund Ranch Road or Sunset Creek Lane. The intersection
closest to the Alisal Property is a three-way intersection of Alisal Street, Minnie Street, and
Sycamore Road, and you state that that intersection was not studied as part of the Project's EIR
because it is too far away from the Project's street connections at Sunset Creek Lane and Lund
Ranch Road to warrant consideration.

With respect to the parking, the Project's 43 single-family homes would each have a
driveway and a garage, and there is street parking available within the Project area. You state that
the City does not anticipate that additional parking needs resultant from the Project will affect areas
outside the Project's boundaries.

File No. A-17-048
Page No. 6

The Project site is currently vacant and consists of open space, and the Project's
construction and operation will generally increase the intensity of use of the City's roads and
infrastructure in the immediate area surrounding the Project. However, with respect to the Project's
effect on the local environment, the EIR concluded that the project would not result in any
significant environmental impacts that could not be mitigated to a less-than-significant level with
recommended mitigation measures. (EIR, supra, 2-2.) Furthermore, the Project preserves 172 of its
total 195 acres as open space.

With respect to the Project's effect on the view from the Alisal Property, the dominate
feature in the foreground of the existing view is a City water tank, and the construction of the
Project will not affect that view.

Regarding the Project's effect on the Alisal Property's privacy, at least three intervening
parcels, Minnie Street, and Sycamore Creek Way all separate the Alisal Property from the
southwest corner of the Project, thereby providing the Alisal Property a privacy buffer.

The EIR concluded that the Project would result in temporary noise increases and air-quality
reductions due to the construction of the Project, and permanent noise increases and air-quality
reductions from the operation of the Project's residences. However, the EIR also concluded that all
noise and air-quality impacts from the Project, including those related to odor, would be less than
significant with mitigation.

Therefore, the decision regarding the Project's zoning would not change the character of the
Alisal Property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use of property surrounding the
Alisal Property.

Market Value

Finally, we consider whether the decision regarding the Project's zoning would affect or
influence the market value of the Alisal Property.

As noted above, there is no indication that the decision would substantially affect the
development potential of the Alisal Property, or alter traffic levels or intensity of use of property
surrounding it. Furthermore, a local real estate broker advised the Commission in writing that the
Project will not impact the value of the Alisal Property. Therefore, the decision regarding the
Project's zoning would not affect or influence the market value of the Alisal Property.

Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in the
decision regarding the Project's compliance with the Ordinance's Planned Unit Development Plan
zoning for the Project because that decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the Commissioner's source-of-income interest in the Alisal Property.

File No. A-17-048
Page No. 7

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Matthew P. Christy
       Counsel, Legal Division

MFCjgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Balch from taking part in the governmental decision on 
whether the GHC Lund Ranch II Project's tentative map complies with the City's Planned Unit 
Development Plan zoning for the Project given that a source of income to the Commissioner owns 
real property nearby the Project?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in that decision because it 
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioner's source-of­
income interest in real property nearby the Project.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have 
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. 
Section 87103(c) provides that an official has an interest in any source of income 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, or received by, the official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made.2 Regulation 18700. l (a)(2) provides that, in 
addition to a source-of-income interest in any business entity from which the official has received 
income of $500 or more within the 12 months before the relevant decision is made, the official also 
has a source-of-income interest in any individual who has the power to direct or cause the direction 
of the management and policies of the business entity. 
5 Gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms available 
to the public without regard to official status are not source-of-income interests under the Act. (Section 87103(c).)  
You state that the Commissioner has received more than $500 of income in the prior 12 
months from a business entity, and that Shennan Balch has the power to direct, or cause the 
direction of, that business entity's management and policies. Based on these facts, the 
Commissioner has a source-of-income interest in Shennan Balch as an individual. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The effect of a decision before the official's agency is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the official's interest is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) An 
official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if it is a named party in, or the subject of, the 
decision. (Ibid.) For an interest not explicitly involved in a decision, the decision's effect is 
reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
The Commissioner's source-of-income interest in Shennan Balch is not explicitly involved 
in the decision regarding the Project's zoning because neither Shennan Balch nor the Alisa} 
Property are a named party in, or a subject of, that decision. Therefore, the effect of the decision is 
reasonably foreseeable if it is a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.3 provides that a decision's effect on a source ofincome is material if the 
official knows or has reason to know that the decision will have a material financial effect on a real­
property interest of the individual source of income under the real-property materiality standards set 
forth in Regulation 18702.2. 
As is pertinent to the facts presented, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably 
foreseeable effect of a decision on a real-property interest is material if the effect: 
"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel of real 
property; ... 
"(10) Would change the character of the of the parcel ofreal property 
by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, 
noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would 
affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a 
financial interest; ... 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental 
decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would 
influence the market value of the official's property." 
Therefore, the Act would prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in the decision if any of these 
three real-property materiality standards is met with respect to the Commissioner's source-of­
income interest in the Alisa! Property. 


Development Potential  
We first examine whether the decision would affect the development potential of the Alisa} 
Property. You state that Sherman Balch currently has the right to develop 1 home per 2 acres on the 
western portion of the Alisa} Property under the City's Happy Valley Specific Plan. 
The Planning Commission's decision on whether the Project's tentative map complies with 
the Ordinance's Planned Unit Development Plan zoning for the Project will not affect the 
development potential of the Alisa} Property. The Ordinance's zoning is solely applicable to the 
Project, and does not affect the Alisa} Property's zoning. Furthermore, you also state that, in the 
future, if Sherman Balch seeks more density with respect to the Alisal Property than is currently 
authorized under the Happy Valley Specific Plan, he would need to obtain amendments to the 
General Plan, Happy Valley Specific Plan, and the zoning applicable to the Alisa! Property. 
Furthermore, infrastructure improvements arising undertaken for the Project also will not 
affect the development potential of the Alisal Property. Although the EIR notes that the Project will 
extend Sunset Creek Way and Lund Ranch Road and utilities to the Project site, it also provides that 
44[r]oad and infrastructure improvements would not extend to any undeveloped properties and 
thereby, not induce new development." The extension of Sunset Creek Way and Lund Ranch Road 
to the Project will primarily serve Project residents, those roads are both multiple residential blocks 
north of the Alisal Property, and there are more convenient routes available to drive to and from the 
Alisa! Property. And the Alisal Property is served by existing utility lines and water tanks that are 
not associated with the Project. 
Therefore, based on the facts presented, the decision regarding the Project's zoning will not 
affect the development potential of the Alisa! Property. 
Traffic Levels and Intensity of Use 
We now examine whether the decision regarding the Project's zoning would change the 
character of the Alisa! Property by altering traffic levels or intensity of use of property surrounding 
the Alisa! Property, including parking, the view, privacy, noise levels, air quality, including odors, 
or any other factors that would affect the market value of the Alisa] Property. 
The Project's EIR concluded that the impact of traffic increases resultant from the Project 
would be less than significant with planned mitigation, and a driver going to or from the Alisa] 
Property does not need to use either Lund Ranch Road or Sunset Creek Lane. The intersection 
closest to the Alisa} Property is a three-way intersection of Alisa] Street, Minnie Street, and 
Sycamore Road, and you state that that intersection was not studied as part of the Project's EIR 
because it is too far away from the Project's street connections at Sunset Creek Lane and Lund 
Ranch Road to warrant consideration. 
With respect to the parking, the Project's 43 single-family homes would each have a 
driveway and a garage, and there is street parking available within the Project area. You state that 
the City does not anticipate that additional parking needs resultant from the Project will affect areas 
outside the Project's boundaries.  
The Project site is currently vacant and consists of open space, and the Project's 
construction and operation will generally increase the intensity of use of the City's roads and 
infrastructure in the immediate area surrounding the Project. However, with respect to the Project's 
effect on the local environment, the EIR concluded that the project would not result in any 
significant environmental impacts that could not be mitigated to a less-than-significant level with 
recommended mitigation measures. (EIR, supra, 2-2.) Furthermore, the Project preserves 172 of its 
total 195 acres as open space. 
With respect to the Project's effect on the view from the Alisa! Property, the dominate 
feature in the foreground of the existing view is a City water tank, and the construction of the 
Project will not affect that view. 
Regarding the Project's effect on the Alisal Property's privacy, at least three intervening 
parcels, Minnie Street, and Sycamore Creek Way all separate the Alisal Property from the 
southwest comer of the Project, thereby providing the Alisa! Property a privacy buffer. 
The EIR concluded that the Project would result in temporary noise increases and air-quality 
reductions due to the construction of the Project, and permanent noise increases and air-quality 
reductions from the operation of the Project's residences. However, the EIR also concluded that all 
noise and air-quality impacts from the Project, including those related to odor, would be less than 
significant with mitigation. 
Therefore, the decision regarding the Project's zoning would not change the character of the 
Alisa! Property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use of property surrounding the 
Alisa} Property. 
Market Value 
Finally, we consider whether the decision regarding the Project's zoning would affect or 
influence the market value of the Alisal Property. 
As noted above, there is no indication that the decision would substantially affect the 
development potential of the Alisal Property, or alter traffic levels or intensity of use of property 
surrounding it. Furthermore, a local real estate broker advised the Commissioner in writing that the 
Project will not impact the value of the Alisa} Property. Therefore, the decision regarding the 
Project's zoning would not affect or influence the market value of the Alisa! Property. 
Thus, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in the 
decision regarding the Project's compliance with the Ordinance's Planned Unit Development Plan 
zoning for the Project because that decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on the Commissioner's source-of-income interest in the Alisa! Property.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Commissioner Balch from taking part in the governmental decision on 
whether the GHC Lund Ranch II Project's tentative map complies with the City's Planned Unit 
Development Plan zoning for the Project given that a source of income to the Commissioner owns 
real property nearby the Project?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Commissioner from taking part in that decision because it 
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Commissioner's source-of­
income interest in real property nearby the Project.

File No. A-17-048 Page No. 2 FACTS You are the Assistant City Attorney and the authorized representative of Commissioner Balch, and you provided additional facts on March 6, 2017. The City Council approved the GHC Lund Ranch II Project (the "Project") in January 2016. As approved, the Project, located at 1500 Lund Ranch Road, would include 43 homes on 195 acres, with 172 of those acres preserved as open space. Also in January 2016, the City Council enacted Ordinance No. 2133 (the "Ordinance"), establishing Planned Unit Development Plan zoning solely applicable to the Project's real-property parcels. The City Council determined that the Project's zoning was in conformance with the City's General Plan when it adopted the Ordinance. The City's Planning Commission will soon consider whether the Project's tentative map complies with the Ordinance's zoning for the real-property parcels within the Project. You state that Commissioner Balch has received more than $500 of income in the prior 12 months from a business entity, and that Sherman Balch, the Commissioner's uncle, has the power to direct, or cause the direction of, that business entity's management and policies.
